did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper analyzes various deep learning and machine learning algorithms, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Fully Convolutional Networks (FCN), MobileNet, ResNet, Support Vector Machines (SVM), KNN, and Decision Trees (DT), to detect and classify dermatological issues and skin cancers (such as melanoma) using datasets like ISIC 2019 and HAM10000.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is presented at the 10th International Conference on Advanced Computing and Communication Systems (ICACCS) and reports original experimental results. It analyzes specific CNN algorithms (CNN, FCN, MobileNet, ResNet) and machine learning models (SVM, KNN, DT) on datasets like ISIC 2019 and HAM10000, providing specific accuracy metrics (e.g., 95%, 97%) and comparing their performance to conclude that CNN is the most effective method. Although it reviews prior literature in the introduction and references section, its primary contribution is the comparative analysis of algorithm performance on dermatological data.

title: Analysis of Different CNN Algorithms to Detect Dermatological Issues
journal: 2024 10th International Conference on Advanced Computing and Communication Systems (ICACCS)
brief_summary: This study analyzes various deep learning and machine learning algorithms, including CNN, FCN, MobileNet, ResNet, SVM, KNN, and Decision Trees, to detect and classify dermatological issues and skin cancers. The models were evaluated using the ISIC 2019 and HAM10000 datasets to assess their performance in identifying skin lesions. The evaluation approach involved comparing accuracy metrics across different architectures and feature extraction techniques. Key results indicated that CNN-based methods generally outperformed traditional machine learning classifiers, with specific models achieving accuracies of up to 97% on the HAM10000 dataset. The analysis concluded that Convolutional Neural Networks are the most effective algorithms for this task due to their superior precision and accuracy in classifying skin disease images.

lesion_types_studied.details: Identified as a prevalent and challenging condition to diagnose; one of the two main categories of skin cancer (along with non-melanoma). Used in classification tasks on ISIC 2019 and HAM10000 datasets.

paper_terms: Classification of Melanoma kinds into Cancerous and Benign
lesion_types_studied.details: Specific sub-task mentioned in Table I using Deep Convulational Neural Networks (DCNN) with 80% accuracy.

all_task_types.target: Dermatological issues, skin diseases, and skin cancer types (e.g., Melanoma vs. Non-melanoma, Cancerous vs. Benign, specific lesion classes like NV, BCC, AK, BKL, DF, VASC, SCC).
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on classifying skin diseases and cancers using CNN, FCN, MobileNet, ResNet, SVM, KNN, and DT. It reports accuracy metrics for categorizing images into discrete diagnostic labels (e.g., 'Classification of Melanoma kinds into Cancerous and Benign', 'Detecting Skin Conditions And Skin Lesions'). Table I lists frameworks used for 'Classification' or 'Detection' (in the context of class identification) with accuracy scores ranging from 74% to 98.90%.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A public dataset used for validating models in the study, containing dermatological imagery.

use.training: Used for validating models; specific split counts not reported.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset used for validating models in the study, containing dermatological imagery. The paper notes it outperformed ISIC 2019 when using the described approach.

use.training: Used for validating models; specific split counts not reported.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy and precision metrics without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2019.notes: The paper reports that SVM, KNN, and DT achieved overall performance of 95%, 94%, and 93% respectively on the ISIC 2019 dataset using a method involving hair removal, filtering, segmentation, and GLCM feature extraction.

HAM10000.notes: The paper reports that SVM, KNN, and DT achieved overall performance of 97%, 95%, and 95% respectively on the HAM10000 dataset. The authors note that the HAM10000 dataset outperformed the ISIC 2019 dataset when this approach was used.

General/Other Studies (Table I).notes: Table I lists accuracy results from various referenced studies. The paper concludes that the FCN-Based Dense Net Framework performed the best among the listed frameworks with 98.90% accuracy. It also notes that CNN-based methods generally outperformed traditional machine learning classifiers.

architecture_used.main_model: The paper analyzes multiple architectures including Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Fully Convolutional Networks (FCN), MobileNet, ResNet, Support Vector Machines (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Decision Trees (DT). Specific models cited in Table I include Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNN), MobileNet Framework, ResNet152V2, FCN-Based Dense Net Framework, ResNet v2 with Adam optimizer, VGG16, and InceptionV3.
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet152V2 was trained using the ImageNet dataset. MobileNetV2 with LSTM was compared against simple MobileNetV2. Other models' pretraining details are not explicitly reported in the provided text.

architecture_details.CNN: Described as having a robust intellectual foundation including load communication, localized understanding, and time domain/down-sampling space. Auto-organization approaches are used to recognize features.
architecture_details.FCN-Based Dense Net: Innovative structure with separate networks connected through skip routes integrating long skip and short-cut connections for residual learning. Includes Conditional Random Field (CRF) for contour refinements. Phases include Encoder-Decoder segmentation, Residual Learning Strategy, Integration with CRF, Densenet Framework, Concatenation Strategy with transition layer, and Regularizer strategy.
architecture_details.MobileNet: Lightweight network topology designed for hardware with limited resources. Uses LeakyReLU function for activation. Avoids decreasing geographic granularity of feature maps and increasing fields of reception (unlike pooling layers).
architecture_details.ResNet: Incorporates residual training units with alternative connections to prevent degradation. ResNet152V2 used for feature extraction.
architecture_details.DCNN: Identifies lesions automatically based on high-level characteristics without prior separation process. Divides melanoma into cancerous and benign classes.
architecture_details.SVM/KNN/DT: Traditional machine learning classifiers used with features extracted via hair removal, filtering (Black-Hat, inpainting, Gaussian), segmentation (Grabcut), and GLCM.

training_details.optimizer: Adam optimizer used for ResNet v2 architecture.
training_details.loss_function: A new loss function was proposed for the MobileNet framework to eliminate intra-class feature variance and improve inter-class feature variations using mutual knowledge and cross-entropy.
training_details.activation_function: LeakyReLU used in the MobileNet framework.
training_details.hyperparameters: Not explicitly reported, though 'Regularizer strategy and Hyper-parameters optimization' is listed as a phase in the FCN-based Dense-Net Framework.

classification_or_output_head.SVM/KNN/DT: Used for classification of extracted features (color, texture, shape) from skin imagery.
classification_or_output_head.CNN/DCNN: Used for disease categorization and diagnosis, classifying skin conditions as harmless, cancerous, or normal.
classification_or_output_head.MobileNet: Classifies skin conditions with reported accuracy of 94.76% in one cited study.
classification_or_output_head.FCN-Based Dense Net: Performs separation and categorization of lesions on the skin.

preprocessing_techniques: Feature extraction (Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM), 2D Wavelet Transform)
Feature extraction (color, texture, and shape characteristics)
conclusions: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) are concluded to be the most effective algorithm for detecting dermatological issues and skin malignancies in terms of evaluation, precision, and accuracy.

Traditional machine learning classifiers (SVM, KNN, DT) achieved overall performance accuracies of 95%, 94%, and 93% respectively on the ISIC 2019 dataset, and 97%, 95%, and 95% on the HAM10000 dataset.
The HAM10000 dataset outperformed the ISIC 2019 dataset when the described preprocessing and classification approach was used.
Among the deep learning frameworks analyzed in Table I, the FCN-Based Dense Net Framework performed the best with an accuracy of 98.90%.

MobileNet-based frameworks achieved high accuracy (94.6% to 94.76%) and are suitable for mobile applications due to their lightweight topology, avoiding the decrease in geographic granularity associated with pooling layers.
ResNet architectures, particularly Inception ResNetV2 with Adam optimizer, achieved 97.1% accuracy, leveraging residual training units to prevent degradation in deep neural networks.

Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNN) can identify lesions automatically based on high-level characteristics without prior separation processes, achieving approximately 80% accuracy in classifying melanoma into cancerous and benign classes.
The study suggests that CNN-based methods generally outperform traditional machine learning classifiers and some cutting-edge techniques for classifying skin diseases.
important_remarks: The paper is a literature review and comparative analysis of existing studies rather than an original experimental study with new primary data collection or novel model training.

Performance metrics (e.g., SVM 95% on ISIC 2019, FCN-Based Dense Net 98.90%) are aggregated from various cited references (e.g., [1], [4], [15], [21]) rather than derived from a unified experimental setup in this specific paper.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets beyond the cited ISIC 2019 and HAM10000 datasets; results are dependent on the original authors' reported figures.
No statistical significance testing (p-values, confidence intervals) is reported for the comparisons between algorithms.
The conclusion that CNN is the 'most effective' algorithm is a generalization based on a survey of multiple disparate studies with varying preprocessing, data splits, and evaluation protocols.
The paper does not report specific data leakage risks or detailed train/validation/test splits for the aggregated results, relying on the integrity of the cited sources.
Clinical relevance is limited as the paper focuses on algorithmic accuracy metrics rather than clinical utility, diagnostic workflow integration, or prospective validation.
